Mutex only used for pointer state, and actual writes happen lock free to avoid contention, deadlocks, bottlenecks. static ZYGISK_LOGD: AtomicI32 = AtomicI32::new(-1)  and // SAFETY: zygisk client code runs single threaded -> avoid mutex because it runs single threaded, atomic used for safe publication/reset. multi-producer + single-consumer architecture: many threads call magisk_log_to_pipe and new_daemon_thread creates logging thread. // We don't need to care the written len because we are writing less than PIPE_BUF // It's guaranteed to always write the whole thing atomically -> kernel synchronisation trick to ensure atomicity, avoids mutex, multiple threads can write concurrently. purpose: Shared task dispatch and worker lifecycle coordination for daemon thread pool

thread_context: Producer threads enqueue work concurrently, worker daemon threads compete for shared task slot, core threads persist while extra threads self-terminate after idle timeout

evidence: static THREAD_POOL: LazyLock<ThreadPool> = LazyLock::new(ThreadPool::default) -> single shared thread pool, initialised once and shared accross threads, ThreadPool: task_is_some: Condvar, task_is_none: Condvar, info: Mutex<PoolInfo> -> shared state. idle_threads = how many workers sleeping right now, total_threads = how many workers exist total. mutex: shared scheduler metadata

purpose: The main event loop which performs I/O on the pseudoterminal and rendering coordination
what_is_shared: Shared terminal state, event notifications, queued messages
why_shared: thread updates terminal from shell output while UI/render thread reads and renders safely without starvation
access_pattern: PTY thread acquires fair lease, attempts try_lock_unfair, falls back to blocking lock. UI sends commands asynchronously through channel
coordination_type: Fair mutex synchronisation + message-passing + event wakeup signaling
thread_context: The PTY thread polls the PTY for readable/writable events, reads shell output, parses escape sequences, updates shared terminal state, wakes renderer. PTY event-loop thread, UI/render thread, input/notifier threads
pattern_observations: Shared-memory for terminal state, message-passing for commands, fairness controls to prevent renderer starvation under heavy PTY throughput
evidence:
terminal: Arc<FairMutex<Term<U>>> -> shared terminal, uses the FairMutex from the other file to ensure fairness, arc: shared ownership across threads -> render and event loop thread both need access. Terminal output stream can be agressive, which is likely why fairmutex was needed. let _terminal_lease = Some(self.terminal.lease()) -> gets lock from fairmutex, preventing other threads from repeatedly jumping ahead, does not lock the actual data yet. match self.pty.reader().read(...) -> happens before terminal lock, because terminal lock is shared and expensive, so io may be blocking -> never hold shared terminal lock during OS reads. never hold shared terminal lock during OS reads -> try acquire terminal state, if the UI thread is rendering -> PTY thread continues buffering data -> renderer finishes -> responsiveness improves. None if unprocessed >= READ_BUFFER_SIZE =>
    self.terminal.lock_unfair() -> caps memory growth. MAX_LOCKED_READ = for preventing PTY thread monopolising terminal lock forever. poller: Arc<Poller> -> Shared across threads, immutable shared coordination, not data-sharing.
notes: One dedicated PTY/event-loop thread owns I/O, while terminal state is shared with the renderer through a fair mutex. Nicely works with other file

purpose: The entire file is about controlling shared-memory access fairness, prevent starvation
what_is_shared: Shared data T plus a separate shared next controlling which thread may gets data next
why_shared: Prevent aggressive threads from repeatedly reacquiring the mutex and starving waiting threads under contention
access_pattern: Threads first acquire next, then acquire data. _next stays alive during data.lock() to serialise contention + optional unfair fast paths bypass fairness
coordination_type: Layered mutex coordination
thread_context: Multi-threaded scenarios where many threads repeatedly compete for the same shared resource
pattern_observations: Shared mutable state is minimised, the extra mutex coordinates scheduling rather than protecting data.  Split between reservation (next) and ownership of data. Supports both fair and unfair locking modes for throughput vs fairness tradeoffs.
evidence: pub struct FairMutex<T> with data: Mutex<T>, next: Mutex<()> -> data protectrs the real shared memory (the actual object), next is for coordination, exists to control the order of lock aquisition. In lock: before it goes to the data, every thread must first aquire the next, so threads queue at next, preventing starvation. _next remains alive while acquiring data, it is dropped after aquiring data. lease() lets a thread reserve future access without locking the data, so the data reservation is separated from the data access. unfair bypasses fairness and just accesses self.data.lock -> allows control, lower overhead, higher throughput, possible starvation. parking_lot mutexes used -> faster, smaller, more flexible than std mutexes -> necessary because this design adds an extra mutex acquisition per lock. In this file, a mutex is guarding another mutex

purpose: Async request/response coordination between debugger transport tasks

evidence: pending_requests: Mutex<HashMap<u64, Sender<Result<Response>>>> -> for each request, a channel is stored so that its response can be delivered later. Arc::new(Self { id, pending_requests: Mutex::new(HashMap::default()), }); -> Arc for shared ownership across async tasks (so multiple spawned tasks like recv/send/err can access the same Transport instance safely without copying it). Option<Arc<syntax::config::LanguageConfiguration>> -> documents share same config, clone again used to increment reference counter. Arc<SavePoint> many threads can hold savepoint, used for revert: Mutex<Transaction>, savepoints are updated as edits happen. self.savepoints.push(Arc::downgrade(&savepoint)), Weak<SavePoint> -> avoids memory leaks, document does not own savepoint, savepoint will dissapear automatically

purpose: for caching, initialise once, thread safe
primitives: RwLock, OnceLock, Box, LazyLock, Arc (tests), AtomicUsize (tests), Barrier (tests)
what_is_shared: map of keys + lazily initialised values (HashMap<K, Box<OnceLock<V>>>)
why_shared: Avoid repeating expensive computation + safely sharing initialised values across threads and minimising lock contention
access_pattern: Read-heavy: RwLock for existing keys, write access only during first insertion, once-only initialisation through OnceLock
coordination_type: Lock based coordination with initialisation once per key
thread_context: Multi-threaded concurrent callers accessing shared cache entries
pattern_observations: Given a key, you can compute a value once, store it, and let all threads reuse the same initialised value. Separates coordination from initialisation: RwLock protects cache structure while OnceLock serialises initialisation per key. Initialization occurs outside map lock to reduce contention and improve scalability. boxed OnceLock ensures stable memory addresses during HashMap rehashing
evidence: pub struct KeyedLazyLock<K, V, F = fn(&K) -> V>, map: LazyLock<RwLock<HashMap<K, Box<OnceLock<V>>>>>, -> hashmap ensures one entry per key, oncelock means that the value starts out as uninitialised, then the first thread initialises it, and later all other threads reuse that same initilised value (cell.get_or_init(|| (self.init)(key));), normally hahsmap entries can move in memory during rehash, so they put it in the box because the box allocation lives on the heap, and heap address stays stable, so then the code can grab the pointer to oncelock, release the map lock, and initialise the outside lock (address needs to stay stable for this), rwlock is used because many threads use mostly read and writes are rare, lazylock so that it delays the creation of the map until first use -> optimisation (allocate when needed). get function: try to acquire read lock and see if key exists, then get pointer to oncelock, initialise outside lock -> avoid holding lock during computation -> avoid blocking. If the key is absent self.map.write(); aquire write lock and insert oncelcok for key, release lock and initialise later. Tests use AtomicUsize to count initialisations, Arc, Barrier: all threads race simultaneously initializes_once_per_key, checks if the synchronisation works correctly
notes: Coordination-heavy cache, concurrent reads, safe lazy initialisation

purpose: "Virtual Machine Monitor that leverages the Linux Kernel-based Virtual Machine (KVM), and other virtualization features to run a single lightweight micro-virtual machine (microVM)." Owns the Vmm struct, KVM VM, device manager, vCPU threads, coordinates lifecycle, snapshotting, device updates. Runs inside an event loop model
what_is_shared: KVM-backed VM state (Arc<Vm>), guest memory mappings (GuestMemoryMmap via Vm), vCPU handles (Arc/shared channels), seccomp filter (Arc<BpfProgram>), device manager state (DeviceManager inside Vmm), event loop subscriber (Arc<Mutex<Vmm>>)
why_shared: Shared because KVM VM, guest memory must be accessible by multiple vCPU threads + device threads, and coordination state must be mutated by event loop
access_pattern: Read mostly shared state, vCPU threads execute independently but synchronise via channels, guest memory accessed concurrently without Rust locks (via mmap/KVM)
coordination_type: event-driven, message passing (channels), barrier synchronisation, KVM + seccomp
thread_context: Main VMM thread (pub struct Vmm) -> API commands, device orchestration, vCPU lifecycle control, event loop. vCPU threads (vcpu.start_threaded) -> Each vCPU runs in its own OS thread, executes code via KVM ioctl loop, communicates back via channels. EventManager thread(type EventManager) -> event loop, dispatches I/O + device + vCPU events, worker threads for I/O
pattern_observations: Separation between control plane and execution plane, no locking for guest execution, kernel is the concurrency boundary, barriers,seccomp applied per thread, channels used for vCPU command/response instead of shared mutation
evidence: GuestMemoryMmap: host maps a region of memory -> KVM shares it with guest -> all vCPU threads see same RAM. pub type EventManager = BaseEventManager<Arc<Mutex<dyn MutEventSubscriber>>>: central event loop, waits for eventfds, timers, device I/O, vCPU exit signals. pub vm: Arc<Vm> : Vm includes GuestMemory, MMIO mappings, once RAM created shared across all threads. pub fn get_mmds(&self) -> Option<Arc<Mutex<Mmds>>>: arc mutex because only 1 thread owns the event loop at a time, still needs shared access to Vmm state + safe mutation during events. vcpu_seccomp_filter: Arc<BpfProgram>:  Linux syscall filter program, used to restrict syscalls in vCPU threads, arc because multiple vCPU threads use same filter (prevents copying). let barrier = Arc::new(Barrier::new(vcpu_count + 1)): vCPU creation, synchronisation -> vCPU threads must fully initialise, main thread must wait before continuing. self.vcpus_handles.push(vcpu.start_threaded ...            self.vcpus_handles.push(vcpu.start_threaded(&self.vm, vcpu_seccomp_filter.clone(), barrier.clone(),)?): spawn OS thread, clone to increment count. barrier.wait() -> synchronisation
notes: Avoids shared-memory concurrency
